story:
Alden Carter sits in his wheelchair at the window, Lake Michigan glittering as summer boats bob on the waves. His wife, Millie, comes from the kitchen with tuna sandwiches, a bowl of salad, and hot coffee. They talk about their daughter, Mara, and her husband, David, who are building an antique business—long hours, but Mara likes to work, and David is a good man. At the table, Alden’s hand shakes so badly that his sandwich slips from his grasp. Frustration flashes; despair follows. Millie quietly feeds him, and he weeps, ashamed at being cared for like an infant. She holds him and reminds him that eight years ago she was the one who couldn’t sit up, who needed feeding; helplessness is hard, but love carried them both then and will now. He tells her she recovered; he will not. He fears the years left to him, fears becoming bedridden. She promises to stay with him, whatever time remains.

Back at the window, watching the boats, Alden wonders where his life went. Disease is taking him piece by piece. He thinks about the moment when he won’t be able to leave the bed and makes a hard private vow: if it comes to that, he will end things himself. Then, like a breath escaping, he lets out a wish as old as sorrow—to be young again, simply healthy again.

That night he dreams of a faceless figure in a mirror. The voice says he has been summoned by that wish, a wish “monumental in nature” and not to be taken lightly. Alden repeats it anyway: he is dying; there is no cure; he wants the strength and ease of his healthiest years. The figure agrees—done. Alden hesitates; what about Millie? She would not want to be left behind, and she too would choose health. The figure allows it: when they wake, both will be as they were at their healthiest.

The next day Mara and David arrive after work and get no answer. At the door, Mara raises a finger for silence. “Don’t wake them,” she whispers. “Two babies are sleeping in Mom and Dad’s bed.” They search the apartment and find, to David’s shock, two babies sleeping in Alden and Millie’s bed. There is no sign of the elderly couple. The police investigate the disappearance until the trail goes cold. The babies, however, are very real. Mara and David alert the authorities and, with permission, take the infants home while the mystery is sorted out. No relatives emerge; no explanation arrives. In time, paperwork catches up to the wonder, and the young couple become the legal parents of the two babies who, in every way that matters, are Mara’s own parents renewed.

They discover they like diapers and midnight feedings more than they expected. The present, full of small hands and bright eyes, is good. Yet even as they laugh at the strange turn of fate—raising their parents—questions hover. What memories, if any, will those babies carry forward? What will it mean when the children grow into names they once wore? The future is going to be—well, you can imagine.
